subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree from a four-year university
Major likely in Supply Chain Management, Business Administration, or a related field
Completed coursework in data analysis, logistics, and operations management
No postgraduate education yet, as currently early in career
Participated in at least one internship related to supply chain during undergraduate studies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Supply Chain Manager (Entry Level)
Primary role involves reviewing and validating supply chain data reports
Responsible for identifying data inconsistencies and reporting them to management
Supports daily operations by ensuring data accuracy in supply chain metrics

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the supply chain management sector
Likely employed in manufacturing, retail, or distribution/logistics
Focuses on inventory, procurement, and logistics data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed by a medium to large-sized company with multiple supply chain operations
Works in a collaborative office environment with a direct reporting line to a manager
Company likely has established processes and structured data reporting tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Operates within a business-to-business (B2B) environment
Company involved in product distribution or manufacturing
Supply chain processes are critical to business operations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Less than 1 year of experience as a Supply Chain Manager
Started in current full-time role immediately after completing undergraduate degree
Currently in the first year of post-graduate employment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Entry-level professional
Holds a junior management position within the supply chain department
Reports directly to a more senior manager
Responsible for routine and operational tasks rather than strategic decision-making

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Downloads supply chain performance reports, primarily from Tableau
Uploads and maintains data in Google Sheets for the team
Checks data for errors or anomalies and flags inconsistencies
Communicates any identified issues directly to manager each morning
Updates and prepares reports for management distribution daily

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 70% of the workday reviewing and preparing reports
About 15% of the time is spent updating data in Google Sheets
Roughly 10% of time is devoted to communicating findings to manager
Remaining time allocated to miscellaneous tasks and process improvements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has autonomy to identify and flag data discrepancies independently
Final decisions or actions based on findings are made by direct manager
Limited authority for process changes; suggestions are routed through management
Operates within established SOPs and reporting guidelines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Downloading and accessing reports from Tableau is routine and straightforward
Uploading data to Google Sheets is quick and familiar
Identifying obvious data errors (e.g., missing values, clear outliers) is relatively easy
Communicating routine findings to the manager is handled with confidence

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Distinguishing between genuine data anomalies and acceptable variances can be difficult
Understanding the root cause of certain data inconsistencies requires more experience
Optimizing data workflows or suggesting improvements feels complex due to lack of tenure
Balancing accuracy with speed, especially under tight deadlines, is challenging

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Daily downloading, uploading, and formatting of reports is highly repetitive
Routine data validation checks could be automated with scripts or AI tools
Transferring data between Tableau, Google Sheets, and Microsoft Excel is labor-intensive
Flagging standard anomalies or missing data is amenable to automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys troubleshooting unique data issues and understanding their impact
Finds it engaging to spot patterns or trends in supply chain reports
Feels bored by repetitive data entry and report formatting tasks
Tediousness arises from manual data transfers and routine checks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual downloading and uploading between platforms is time-consuming
Occasional formatting issues cause delays when importing data to G-sheets
Lack of integration between Tableau and Google Sheets creates extra steps
Identifying subtle data issues sometimes requires more domain knowledge than currently possessed

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher enjoyment and productivity are linked when solving new problems or learning
Feels most productive when tasks are both engaging and within skill set
Repetitive tasks are performed quickly due to familiarity, though with lower enjoyment
Developing new skills in analytics or automation is both enjoyable and seen as a productivity booster

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open and positive about adopting new technologies
Sees technology as a means to improve efficiency and reduce manual workload
Eager to try new tools that can automate repetitive tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers themselves quick to learn new software, especially data-related tools
Experience with regularly picking up updates in Tableau and Google Suite
Adapts well to process changes or new workflow integrations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours per week to learn new tools if they are relevant to daily work
Prefers hands-on, practical training modules or guided tutorials
Sees tech training as beneficial for career progression

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential to reduce repetitive workload and increase efficiency
Sometimes workload pressures make it hard to prioritize training
Needs to see clear relevance to current tasks to justify time investment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Manager encourages the exploration of technologies that improve reporting accuracy
Peers who successfully use new tools inspire adoption and knowledge sharing
Team culture values tech-savviness and rewards initiative in tool adoption

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Tableau daily for accessing and analyzing supply chain reports
Uploads and shares data via Google Sheets as part of daily workflow
Relies on Microsoft Excel for ad hoc data manipulation and formatting
Leverages Google Workspace (Docs, Slides, Gmail) for communication and collaboration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Google Sheets is critical for data sharing and real-time collaboration
Microsoft Excel is used for detailed data analysis and formatting
Email (Gmail/Outlook) used for routine communication with manager and team

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Comfortable with Tableau as a leading analytics platform
Familiar with both Google and Microsoft productivity suites
Aware of ERP systems like SAP or Oracle, but limited hands-on experience
Some exposure to basic scripting or automation tools through online resources

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Manual process needed to transfer data from Tableau to Google Sheets
Occasional formatting errors occur when moving data between Excel and Sheets
Lack of direct integration between reporting and collaboration platforms increases workload
Would benefit from more seamless data pipelines between tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts like machine learning, natural language processing, and automation
Comfortable with terms such as 'large language models' and 'predictive analytics'
Has read introductory articles or taken short courses on AI fundamentals

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses large language model-based chatbots (e.g., ChatGPT or Gemini) daily
Occasionally leverages AI-powered features within Google Workspace (like Smart Compose)
Has experimented with AI-driven data cleaning tools online

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs every day to help draft reports, check data logic, or summarize findings
Relies on AI chatbots to troubleshoot Excel and Tableau formulas
Consults LLMs for recommendations on automating repetitive reporting tasks
Occasionally uses AI to generate template emails or documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI successfully flagged a recurring formula error in a supply chain report, saving time
LLM once misunderstood a prompt and provided irrelevant data-cleaning advice, requiring manual correction
Discovered that AI-generated summaries need to be reviewed for accuracy before sharing with manager
AI tools helped speed up email drafting, but sometimes produced overly generic messages

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal AI training offered by the company yet
Peers occasionally share tips or prompt ideas for LLMs informally
Relies on self-guided learning and online tutorials to improve AI tool use
Manager is supportive of AI experimentation but no structured resources are provided

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools for complex tasks
Uses AI independently for quick lookups or simple automations
Finds a back-and-forth approach effective when troubleshooting workflow problems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as a digital assistant or advisor
Uses AI to supplement personal judgment, not replace it
Sees AI as a 'junior co-worker' that can handle routine or repetitive queries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to incorporating AI-driven steps if they increase efficiency
Eager to experiment with new AI features as they become available
Willing to adjust work habits to integrate AI more deeply into daily routines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming, learning, and troubleshooting
Uses command-based prompts for routine automations or data tasks
Finds natural language interactions lower the barrier to entry for new AI features

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many daily tasks require basic data analysis but are not highly specialized
Flagging non-obvious data issues occasionally needs contextual supply chain knowledge
Relies on manager for interpretation of more nuanced or business-critical data anomalies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine tasks like report drafting or formula checks, with human review
Does not fully rely on AI recommendations for business-critical decisions
Always reviews AI-generated content before sharing with manager

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive, rule-based processes
Values human oversight for tasks involving judgment or business implications
Believes the best results come from combining AI efficiency with human context

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for data transfer, report formatting, or scheduling
Views automation of judgment-based tasks as potentially risky without oversight
Would feel threatened only if automation replaced core analytical responsibilities

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the importance of analytical and problem-solving skills
Sees manual data entry skills as becoming less critical due to automation
Considers adaptability and tech-savviness as increasingly valuable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning prompt engineering for effective LLM use
Monitoring and quality-checking AI-generated outputs
Developing basic skills in scripting or automation tools
Evaluating the impact of AI recommendations on supply chain metrics

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mild concern about automation of highly repetitive reporting tasks
Believes unique analytical and judgment-based work is less likely to be automated
Views AI as a tool to augment, not replace, the role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Maintains manual review checkpoints for any AI-generated data or reports
Always verifies AI recommendations before taking action
Company requires human review of all critical data submissions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company is cautiously open to AI experimentation but lacks formal policies
Team encourages sharing of AI best practices informally
No mandatory AI use, but adoption is increasing organically

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will drive significant efficiency gains in supply chain management
Thinks AI will create new types of jobs focused on oversight and strategy
Acknowledges risks of job displacement in repetitive roles

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports transparent AI systems with clear accountability
Worried about potential for AI to propagate data errors if not monitored
Believes human oversight is essential to mitigate ethical risks
Values privacy and data security in AI-enabled workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Wants AI tools that give actionable recommendations to meet monthly goals
Would benefit from seamless integration between Tableau, Google Sheets, and Excel
Desires AI-driven anomaly detection tailored to supply chain data
Seeks customizable AI workflows for report automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects manual data tasks to be almost fully automated within a decade
Anticipates focusing more on analytical, strategic, and exception-based work
Foresees growing responsibility for managing and validating AI-powered systems
Believes continuous learning of AI tools will be essential for career growth

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Wants the company to provide structured AI training and certification
Hopes for investment in integrating AI into daily reporting workflows
Recommends establishing clear guidelines for human-AI collaboration
Suggests pilot projects to evaluate AI effectiveness before full-scale adoption
